walkthrough.title: Choose a Quest
walkthrough.text: Let's get Descent set up. First, everyone needs to grab the Quest Guide. This book contains all 20 quests in the game, each with unique maps, objectives, and special rules. For your very first game, we strongly recommend choosing the introductory quest called First Blood. It is shorter, simpler, and teaches the basics perfectly. Open the Quest Guide to First Blood and keep it handy because you will refer to it throughout setup.

walkthrough.title: Assemble the Map
walkthrough.text: Now look at the Encounter 1 diagram in the Quest Guide for your chosen quest. You will see a layout showing which map tiles to use and how they fit together. The game includes 48 double-sided puzzle-cut map tiles, with indoor locations on one side and outdoor environments on the other. Find the tiles listed by their tile codes, which are a number and letter combination printed on each tile. Snap them together like a jigsaw puzzle in the center of the table. Place any door tokens in the plastic stands and position them on the map where indicated.

walkthrough.title: Choose Player Roles
walkthrough.text: Now decide who plays the overlord. One player takes the role of the overlord, controlling all the monsters and working against the heroes. Everyone else plays as heroes. The rulebook recommends the most experienced player take the overlord role. If you cannot all agree, determine it randomly by rolling a die. In a two-player game, the hero player controls two heroes. With three players, you have two heroes. With four, three heroes. With five, four heroes. The overlord is always one player.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Token Supply
walkthrough.text: Sort all the tokens into piles by type and place them within easy reach of everyone. You will need 45 damage tokens, 35 fatigue tokens, 16 hero tokens in four colors, 9 search tokens, 6 lieutenant tokens, 10 objective tokens, 8 villager tokens, and 20 condition tokens. Also gather the 7 door tokens, which should already be inserted into their plastic stands. Keep all of these organized in the center of the table.

walkthrough.title: Assemble the Search Deck and Condition Cards
walkthrough.text: Take all 12 Search cards, shuffle them together, and place the deck face down within easy reach of the hero players. These represent items heroes can discover during the quest. Next, sort the 16 Condition cards into separate piles by type: Disease, Immobilize, Poison, and Stun. Place these piles where all players can reach them. When a figure is inflicted with a condition, you will reference the matching Condition card.

walkthrough.title: Hero Players Take Activation Cards and Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each hero player chooses one Activation card and takes the hero tokens of the matching color. There are four colors available. The Activation card has two sides: one side shows the hero turn summary, and the other shows the overlord turn summary. You will flip this card at the end of your turn to show you are done. The hero tokens are useful for tracking effects during the game.

walkthrough.title: Choose Heroes
walkthrough.text: Now hero players choose which hero to play. The game includes 8 Hero sheets and 8 hero figures. The heroes are Jain Fairwood, Tomble Burrowell, Avric Albright, Leoric of the Book, Grisban the Thirsty, Syndrael, Ashrian, and Widow Tarha. Each Hero sheet shows the hero's name, archetype, Speed, Health, Stamina, Defense, four attributes (Might, Knowledge, Willpower, Awareness), a unique hero ability, and a heroic feat. All hero players must agree on who controls which hero. Take your chosen Hero sheet and the matching gray hero figure.

walkthrough.title: Choose Classes
walkthrough.text: Each hero has an archetype printed on their Hero sheet: Warrior, Healer, Mage, or Scout. Your archetype determines which Class decks you can choose from. Each archetype has two classes available. Warriors can be a Knight or Berserker. Healers can be a Disciple or Spiritspeaker. Mages can be a Runemaster or Necromancer. Scouts can be a Thief or Wildlander. Pick one class matching your archetype and take that entire Class deck of cards. No two players may choose the same class. Return unchosen Class decks to the box.

walkthrough.title: Choose Skills and Starting Equipment
walkthrough.text: Look through your Class deck. Every hero begins the game with the basic skill card, which has no experience icon, plus the starting equipment cards for that class. In a basic game, all other skill cards are returned to the game box because those are purchased with experience points during campaigns. Place your basic skill and starting equipment cards face up in front of you next to your Hero sheet. Your starting equipment typically includes a weapon and possibly some armor or other items.

walkthrough.title: Place Heroes on the Map
walkthrough.text: Each hero player places their hero figure on the map in the area indicated by the quest rules for hero setup. This is typically on an entrance tile. Check the Quest Guide for exactly where heroes should start. Make sure each hero figure is in a separate space because figures cannot share spaces.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Chooses Monsters
walkthrough.text: Now the overlord sets up. The Quest Guide lists the monster group options available for the chosen quest. The overlord takes the Act I Monster cards and corresponding plastic figures for the chosen monster groups. The base game includes 31 monster figures across 8 monster types: Zombies (5), Goblin Archers (5), Cave Spiders (5), Flesh Moulders (4), Barghests (4), Ettins (2), Elementals (2), Merriods (2), and Shadow Dragons (2). Each type comes in tan (minion) and red (master) varieties. If the quest features a lieutenant, the overlord also takes the appropriate Act I Lieutenant card and token.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Performs Quest Setup
walkthrough.text: The overlord now refers to the Setup section of the chosen quest in the Quest Guide and follows the instructions. This includes placing monster figures, objective tokens, search tokens, and villager tokens on the map as indicated. Pay close attention to the number of monster figures to place, because it varies based on the number of heroes. The Monster card back shows the group size for each hero count.

walkthrough.title: Create the Overlord Deck
walkthrough.text: The overlord shuffles the 15 basic Overlord cards to create the Overlord deck. In the basic game, all other Overlord cards are returned to the box. These upgrade cards are only used during campaigns or when using the Epic Play variant. Place the Overlord deck face down in front of the overlord player.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Draws Starting Hand
walkthrough.text: The overlord draws a number of Overlord cards from the top of the deck equal to the number of heroes in the game. So with two heroes, draw two cards. With four heroes, draw four. Keep these cards hidden from the hero players. There is no hand limit for Overlord cards. You are now ready to play.

walkthrough.title: Place the Dice in the Center
walkthrough.text: Finally, place all 9 custom dice near the center of the table within reach of everyone. The dice include 1 blue attack die, 2 red power dice, 2 yellow power dice, 1 brown defense die, 2 gray defense dice, and 1 black defense die. These dice are shared by all players for attacks, defense rolls, and attribute tests throughout the game. Setup is complete. Let's learn how to play.

walkthrough.title: Round Structure
walkthrough.text: Descent is played over a series of game rounds. Each round, all heroes take their turns first, then the overlord takes a single turn. The hero players decide as a group the order in which they act each round, and this order can change from round to round. If they cannot agree, they go clockwise starting from the player to the left of the overlord. Each player completes their entire turn before the next player begins. After all heroes have finished, the overlord takes their turn. When the overlord finishes, the round ends and a new one begins.

walkthrough.title: Hero Turn Overview
walkthrough.text: Each hero turn has four steps performed in order. First is the Start of Turn, where any effects lasting until the start of your next turn end, you may use start-of-turn abilities, and you refresh all your exhausted cards by rotating them upright. Second is Equip Items, where you can swap which items are equipped. Third is Perform Actions, where you get two actions, which you can perform in any order and even repeat the same action twice. Fourth, you flip your Activation card face down to show your turn is complete.

walkthrough.title: Hero Actions: Move
walkthrough.text: When a hero performs a move action, they receive movement points equal to their Speed. Moving into an adjacent space costs one movement point, though some terrain costs more. Figures cannot move through spaces containing enemy figures or obstacles, which are called blocked spaces. However, you can move diagonally and through friendly figures, though you cannot end your movement in a space with another figure. A key rule: you can interrupt a move action with another action, then finish moving. For example, move two spaces, attack, then move two more. Heroes can also suffer fatigue to gain extra movement points, one fatigue per extra space, even without performing a move action.

walkthrough.title: Hero Actions: Attack
walkthrough.text: To attack, the hero uses one of their equipped weapons to target a space containing a monster. Melee weapons can only target adjacent spaces. Ranged weapons can target any space in line of sight. Combat follows five steps. First, declare your weapon and target. Second, the attacker rolls their attack dice while the defender rolls defense dice simultaneously. Third, for Ranged attacks, check if you rolled enough range to reach the target. Fourth, spend any surge results to trigger special abilities. Fifth, calculate damage: total up all heart results from the attack, subtract shield results from the defense roll, and the remainder is dealt as damage.

walkthrough.title: Hero Actions: Rest, Search, and Others
walkthrough.text: Rest lets the hero recover all fatigue at the end of their turn. Place a hero token on your Activation card as a reminder, then discard all fatigue when you flip the card. Search lets you reveal an adjacent or same-space search token and draw a Search card. Stand Up is the only action a knocked out hero can perform, rolling two red power dice to recover damage and fatigue. Revive a Hero lets you help a knocked out hero in an adjacent space recover by rolling two red dice. Open or Close a Door lets you open or close one adjacent door. Use a Skill lets you activate a Class card ability marked with an action icon. Finally, Special actions come from quest rules, cards, or items.

walkthrough.title: Dice Symbols and Combat Details
walkthrough.text: The dice use specific symbols. Hearts represent damage dealt or recovered. Surges let you trigger special abilities, and one unused surge from an attack can recover one fatigue for heroes. The X on the blue attack die means the attack is a miss and all results are ignored. Numbers on dice represent range for Ranged attacks. Shields on defense dice cancel one damage each. After rolling, players may use abilities that allow rerolls. Each individual surge can trigger one surge ability, and each surge ability can only trigger once per attack. Surge abilities are only used during attacks unless otherwise specified.

walkthrough.title: Attack Keywords: Blast, Pierce, Reach
walkthrough.text: Three important attack keywords appear on weapons and monster cards. Blast means all spaces adjacent to the targeted space are also affected. The attacker rolls once, but each affected figure rolls defense separately. Only the target space needs to be in range and line of sight. Pierce lets an attack ignore a number of shield results equal to the Pierce rating. Pierce from multiple sources stacks. Reach allows a Melee attack to target a figure up to two spaces away instead of only adjacent, as long as the target is in line of sight.

walkthrough.title: Equipment Rules
walkthrough.text: Each hero can carry any number of cards, but equipping them is limited. Heroes have two hand slots, so equipped items with hand icons cannot total more than two hands. A hero can equip only one Armor item and up to two Other items. Unequipped items are flipped face down and cannot be used until equipped. You can only change your equipped items during the Equip Items step at the start of your turn. Heroes can trade Shop Items, Relic cards, and Search cards with adjacent heroes during a move action without spending an action or movement points.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Turn
walkthrough.text: After all heroes have finished, the overlord takes their turn. First, the overlord draws one Overlord card and may play any cards that say they are played at the start of your turn. Then the overlord refreshes exhausted cards. The main phase is activating monsters. The overlord activates each monster group one at a time, in any order. All monsters of one type must finish before moving to the next group. Each monster may perform up to two actions but only one attack per activation. Monster actions include Move, Attack, Monster Action (special abilities on their card), and Open or Close a Door.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Cards
walkthrough.text: Overlord cards represent the overlord's tricks, traps, and powers. The basic deck has 15 cards. There is no cost to play an Overlord card and no limit to how many can be played per turn. Each card specifies when it may be played. Two cards with the same name cannot be played on the same target for the same trigger. After use, the card goes to a face-up discard pile. There is no hand limit. When the deck runs out, the overlord shuffles the discard pile to form a new deck. The overlord draws one card at the start of each turn.

walkthrough.title: Monsters: Minions and Masters
walkthrough.text: Each monster type has minion and master varieties. Minions are tan figures and are the basic version. Masters are red figures and have better stats and stronger abilities. The Monster card shows information for both: characteristics like Speed, Health, and Defense on top for minions and bottom for masters, plus abilities and attack dice. The number of monsters in each group depends on the number of heroes. Some monsters are large, occupying more than one space. Large monsters shrink to one space for movement counting, then expand back when they stop.

walkthrough.title: Damage, Health, Fatigue, and Stamina
walkthrough.text: When a figure suffers damage, place damage tokens on the Hero sheet or near the monster figure. When damage equals or exceeds a figure's Health, it is defeated. Defeated monsters are removed from the map. Defeated heroes are knocked out: replace the figure with a hero token, suffer fatigue up to Stamina, damage up to Health, discard Conditions, and the overlord draws one Overlord card. A knocked out hero can only Stand Up on their next turn. Fatigue is suffered voluntarily to use skills or gain extra movement. If fatigue equals Stamina, no more can be suffered. If forced to take fatigue beyond Stamina, the excess becomes damage instead.

walkthrough.title: Conditions
walkthrough.text: Attacks with a surge ability featuring a condition (Disease, Immobilize, Poison, or Stun) inflict that condition if the attack deals at least one damage after defense. When a hero gets a condition, take the matching Condition card. When a monster gets one, place a condition token nearby. A figure cannot have the same condition twice. Each condition has different effects described on its card. Conditions can be devastating, so pay attention to which monsters and weapons can inflict them.

walkthrough.title: Line of Sight
walkthrough.text: To check line of sight, trace a straight line from any corner of the figure's space to any corner of the target space. If the line passes through a map tile edge, a door, or a blocked space (containing a figure or obstacle), the target is not in line of sight. If the line passes along the edge of a blocked space, line of sight is also blocked. But if the line only touches the corner of a blocked space without passing through it, line of sight is maintained. Adjacent spaces are always in line of sight since they share at least one edge or corner.

walkthrough.title: Terrain Types
walkthrough.text: Map spaces can have special terrain marked by colored borders. Obstacles have red borders and are blocked spaces that stop movement and line of sight. Water has blue borders and costs two movement points to enter instead of one. Pits have green borders. Entering a pit deals two damage, ends your move, and limits your line of sight to adjacent figures only. You need a special action to climb out. Lava has yellow borders. Entering lava deals one damage, and any figure ending its turn on lava is immediately defeated. Normal spaces have no colored border regardless of artwork.

walkthrough.title: Heroic Feats and Attribute Tests
walkthrough.text: Each hero has a unique heroic feat on their Hero sheet. These are powerful, once-per-encounter abilities. After using a heroic feat, flip the Hero sheet face down to mark it as used. It refreshes at the start of the next encounter. Attribute tests come up throughout the game. To test an attribute, roll one gray defense die and one black defense die. If you roll shields equal to or less than your attribute value, you pass. Otherwise, you fail. Monsters always automatically fail attribute tests. Lieutenants have attributes and test normally like heroes.

walkthrough.title: Quests and Encounters
walkthrough.text: Most quests consist of two parts called Encounters. Completing Encounter 1 does not determine the winner. The first side to complete the final encounter's victory condition wins the quest. Between encounters, heroes keep all damage but recover all fatigue, keep conditions, keep search cards, and flip Hero sheets face up for heroic feats. Knocked out heroes stand up for free. The overlord keeps their hand. Then disassemble the Encounter 1 map and set up Encounter 2. Each quest has specific victory conditions for both sides listed in the Quest Guide.

walkthrough.title: The Golden Rules
walkthrough.text: Three golden rules override everything else. First, quest rules in the Quest Guide take precedence over rulebook rules. Cards and abilities take precedence over both the Quest Guide and the rulebook. Second, when two players want to use abilities with the same trigger, the current player decides the order. Third, players are never limited by the physical number of tokens in the box. If you run out of damage, fatigue, or condition tokens, use coins, beads, or any suitable replacement. Always check the Quest Guide first when a rule seems unclear.

walkthrough.title: Welcome to First Blood
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through the opening rounds of the introductory quest First Blood with a recommended three-player game: two hero players and one overlord. Our heroes are Avric Albright, a Healer playing the Disciple class, and Jain Fairwood, a Scout playing the Wildlander class. The overlord is running Goblin Archers and Ettins. We will script out the key moments so you can see how turns, combat, movement, and the overlord all work together.

walkthrough.title: Initial Board State
walkthrough.text: After setup, the map shows a dungeon with two entrance tiles where the heroes start. Avric and Jain place their figures on the entrance spaces. The overlord has placed goblin archers in the first room, with an ettin further down the corridor behind a door. Search tokens are scattered on the map. The overlord has drawn two Overlord cards into his starting hand since there are two heroes.

walkthrough.title: Round 1: Heroes Decide Turn Order
walkthrough.text: The hero players discuss who should go first. Since Jain Fairwood has a high Speed of 5, they decide she should move first to scout ahead. Avric will go second to follow up and support. Remember, the heroes can choose any order each round.

walkthrough.title: Jain's Turn: Move and Attack
walkthrough.text: Jain starts her turn. She refreshes any exhausted cards (none yet) and moves on to actions. She has two actions. For her first action, she performs a Move. Her Speed is 5, so she gets 5 movement points. She moves forward 3 spaces toward a goblin archer. For her second action, she performs an Attack using her Yew Shortbow, a Ranged weapon. She declares the goblin archer as her target. She rolls the blue attack die and one yellow power die. The overlord rolls one gray defense die for the goblin. Jain rolls 3 hearts, 2 range, and 1 surge. The goblin rolls 1 shield. She spends the surge to add +1 heart, totaling 4 hearts minus 1 shield equals 3 damage on the goblin.

walkthrough.title: Why Ranged Attacks Are Powerful
walkthrough.text: Notice Jain did not need to be adjacent to the goblin to attack. Ranged weapons let you target any space in your line of sight, as long as you roll enough range on the dice. Jain rolled 2 range and the goblin was only 2 spaces away, so she hit. If the goblin had been further, she could have spent a surge to add more range. Ranged heroes can deal damage while staying out of harm's way.

walkthrough.title: Avric's Turn: Move and Search
walkthrough.text: Avric goes next. His Speed is 4. For his first action, he moves 4 spaces forward, ending up adjacent to a search token. For his second action, he performs a Search. He flips the search token face up and draws the top card from the Search deck. He gets a Health Potion, which he can use later to recover five damage. He keeps the card near his Hero sheet. Nice find.

walkthrough.title: Overlord's Turn: Draw Card and Activate Goblins
walkthrough.text: Now the overlord takes their turn. First, the overlord draws one Overlord card. Now they have three cards in hand. Then the overlord activates the goblin archer group. Each goblin gets two actions. The first goblin moves toward Jain and attacks with a Ranged attack. The overlord rolls the blue die and a yellow power die. Jain rolls her defense dice as shown on her Hero sheet. The goblin deals 2 damage to Jain after defense. The second goblin moves closer and attacks Avric, dealing 1 damage.

walkthrough.title: End of Round 1
walkthrough.text: After the overlord finishes activating all monster groups, the round ends. Each hero flips their Activation card face up. A new round begins. The heroes discuss their plan for Round 2.

walkthrough.title: Round 2: Jain Attacks Again
walkthrough.text: Jain goes first again. She moves one space to get better positioning and attacks the damaged goblin archer. She rolls 4 hearts. The goblin only had 2 health remaining after last round's damage. The goblin is defeated and removed from the map. First monster down.

walkthrough.title: Avric's Turn: The Mistake — Forgetting to Open the Door
walkthrough.text: Avric wants to push forward toward the objective deeper in the dungeon. He uses both actions to move, covering 8 spaces total. He ends up right next to a closed door. But here is the problem: he cannot see or reach anything on the other side. He used both his actions on movement and forgot that opening a door is an action. He is stuck staring at a closed door with no actions left. His turn is wasted in terms of progress.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Plan Your Actions Around Doors
walkthrough.text: Doors are a big deal in Descent. You cannot move through them, trace line of sight through them, and spaces on opposite sides of a door are not considered adjacent. Opening a door costs one of your two actions. Always plan ahead: if you need to open a door this turn, budget one action for the door and one for moving or attacking. A common beginner mistake is double-moving into a door and wasting the turn.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Plays a Trap Card
walkthrough.text: During the overlord's turn, the overlord activates the remaining goblins to pressure the heroes. Then the overlord plays an Overlord card: a trap card on Jain as she is in a vulnerable position. The trap forces Jain to test an attribute. Jain rolls one gray defense die and one black defense die. She needs to roll shields equal to or less than her Willpower to pass. She rolls 3 shields but her Willpower is only 2. She fails the test and suffers the penalty listed on the card.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Respect the Overlord's Hand
walkthrough.text: The overlord always has cards in hand, and you never know what they are. Trap cards can punish heroes who overextend without support. The overlord draws one card per turn, and there is no hand limit, so cards can accumulate. Heroes should stay within supporting range of each other and not rush blindly into danger. A well-timed Overlord card can turn the tide of an encounter.

walkthrough.title: Round 3: Avric Opens the Door
walkthrough.text: Avric learned from his mistake. This turn he uses his first action to Open the Door. The door token is removed from the map and placed nearby. Now everyone can see and move into the room beyond. The overlord places any new monsters or tokens in the revealed area as specified by the Quest Guide. For his second action, Avric moves into the newly opened room.

walkthrough.title: Jain's Mistake: Ignoring Fatigue Management
walkthrough.text: Jain wants to rush into the new room quickly. She suffers 3 fatigue to gain 3 extra movement points, moving 8 spaces total. She attacks a newly revealed monster. But now she has 3 fatigue on her Hero sheet and her Stamina is 5. If she wants to use her Wildlander skills next turn, most of which cost fatigue, she will be close to her limit. And if she takes more fatigue from game effects, the excess becomes damage. She is now dangerously exposed and exhausted.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Manage Your Fatigue Carefully
walkthrough.text: Fatigue is one of the most important resources in Descent. You spend it to gain extra movement and to activate powerful skills. But your Stamina is the hard cap. Once fatigue equals Stamina, you cannot voluntarily suffer any more. And if a game effect forces fatigue beyond Stamina, it becomes damage instead. Do not burn all your fatigue rushing forward. Leave a cushion so you can use skills when you need them. If you are getting low, consider using the Rest action to recover all fatigue at end of turn.

walkthrough.title: Avric Uses His Heroic Feat
walkthrough.text: Avric is adjacent to Jain, who is wounded and fatigued. This is a perfect time to use his heroic feat. Avric flips his Hero sheet face down and activates his once-per-encounter ability. As a Healer archetype, his feat provides significant healing to nearby heroes. Jain recovers damage and is back in fighting shape. Remember, heroic feats are powerful but one-use per encounter, so time them wisely.

walkthrough.title: Jain's Mistake: Forgetting About Line of Sight
walkthrough.text: Jain wants to shoot the master ettin from behind a pillar. She declares her attack, rolls her dice, and gets a great result. But wait. The overlord challenges the attack because the pillar is an obstacle, and tracing a line from any corner of Jain's space to any corner of the ettin's space passes through the obstacle. The obstacle blocks line of sight. The attack is invalid. Jain has wasted her action.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Always Check Line of Sight First
walkthrough.text: Before declaring a Ranged attack, always verify line of sight to your target. Trace an imaginary line from any corner of your space to any corner of the target space. If it passes through or along the edge of a blocked space, a door, or a map tile edge, the attack is blocked. Touching only the corner of a blocked space is okay. Get in the habit of checking before you commit your action. Moving to a better angle costs less than losing an entire attack action.

walkthrough.title: The Heroes Push Toward the Objective
walkthrough.text: With the door open and lessons learned, the heroes advance methodically. Avric heals and supports while Jain picks off monsters at range. They check line of sight before attacking, manage their fatigue, and coordinate their turn order. The overlord plays Overlord cards strategically and positions monsters to block key corridors. This back-and-forth is the heart of Descent.

walkthrough.title: Encounter Transition
walkthrough.text: Eventually one side meets the victory condition for Encounter 1. If the heroes succeeded, great. If the overlord won Encounter 1, it affects the setup of Encounter 2 but does not end the quest. Between encounters, heroes keep all damage but recover all fatigue. Conditions carry over. Hero sheets flip face up so heroic feats are available again. Knocked out heroes stand up for free. The overlord keeps their hand. Now disassemble the Encounter 1 map and set up Encounter 2 from the Quest Guide. The quest continues.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have seen hero and overlord turns in action, practiced combat with both Melee and Ranged weapons, watched Avric and Jain learn from three common mistakes, and experienced the flow of quest encounters. The key takeaways: plan your actions around doors, manage fatigue as a precious resource, and always check line of sight before attacking. You are ready to play through a full quest on your own.

walkthrough.title: Before You Descend
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full quest, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy advice for both heroes and the overlord.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to every hero, class, monster, condition, terrain type, and game term.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the biggest beginner traps. First, heroes spreading out across the map and getting picked off one by one instead of staying together. Second, forgetting that opening a door costs an action and wasting a turn. Third, the overlord forgetting that each monster only gets one attack per activation, even though it gets two actions. Fourth, heroes blowing all their fatigue on movement and having none left for skills. Fifth, ignoring search tokens. Search cards provide potions and items that can turn the tide of an encounter. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the most common problems.

walkthrough.title: Hero Strategy: Stay Together
walkthrough.text: The single most important hero strategy is to stay together. Descent is a cooperative game for the heroes, and the overlord has a huge advantage against isolated heroes. When heroes cluster, they can revive knocked out allies, trade items, and focus fire on the same monsters. A hero two rooms ahead of the group is a dead hero. Move as a unit, open doors together, and always keep at least one hero within reviving distance of the others.

walkthrough.title: Hero Strategy: Action Economy
walkthrough.text: Each hero gets exactly two actions per turn. Every wasted action matters. Plan your turn before you start executing. Do you need to open a door? Budget one action for it. Need to search? Move adjacent to the search token in one action, search with the other. The move-interrupt rule is powerful: you can move some spaces, perform another action like attacking, then finish your remaining movement. Use this to position yourself optimally both before and after an attack.

walkthrough.title: Hero Strategy: Fatigue Management
walkthrough.text: Fatigue is your most flexible resource. You can suffer fatigue to gain extra movement points or activate powerful skills. But Stamina is a hard limit. Once you hit it, no more voluntary fatigue. Worse, if a game effect forces fatigue beyond Stamina, the excess becomes damage. Good players keep at least one or two fatigue below their Stamina as a buffer. Use the Rest action strategically. Resting recovers all fatigue at end of turn, but it costs one of your two precious actions. Rest when you are safe, not when monsters are closing in.

walkthrough.title: Hero Strategy: Focus Fire
walkthrough.text: Damaged monsters are just as dangerous as healthy ones. A goblin archer with 1 health remaining deals the same damage as one at full health. The most efficient hero strategy is to focus all attacks on one monster at a time until it is defeated and removed from the map. Reducing the overlord's monster count directly reduces the number of attacks the heroes face each round. Do not spread damage across three different monsters. Kill one, then move to the next.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Strategy: Prioritize the Objective
walkthrough.text: New overlord players often focus entirely on killing heroes, but winning a quest is about completing your objective, not getting the most kills. Read your victory conditions carefully. If the objective is to escort a lieutenant off the map, focus your monsters on clearing the path and blocking heroes rather than chasing kills. Knocking out a hero is useful because it wastes their actions on standing up and gives you an extra card, but it is a means to an end, not the end itself.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Strategy: Use Your Cards Wisely
walkthrough.text: Overlord cards are your hidden weapon. You draw one per turn and have no hand limit, so saving cards for critical moments is often better than playing them immediately. Trap cards that force attribute tests can devastate heroes at key chokepoints. Buff cards on your strongest monsters make their attacks lethal. Time your plays to disrupt the heroes' plans at the worst possible moment. Also remember, when a hero is knocked out, you draw an extra card, so aggressive play can snowball.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Strategy: Block with Monsters
walkthrough.text: Heroes cannot move through monster figures. Use your monsters to block doorways, narrow corridors, and access to objectives. A single large monster like an ettin occupying a two-space doorway can force heroes to spend multiple turns dealing with it before they can proceed. Position your minion monsters as speedbumps while your master monsters deal damage. Even a weak goblin archer blocking a critical space buys the overlord one more turn.

walkthrough.title: Understanding Monster Roles
walkthrough.text: Each monster type plays differently. Zombies are slow but tough, good for blocking. Goblin Archers are fast and ranged, great for harassment. Cave Spiders are fragile but numerous and can swarm. Flesh Moulders have Ranged attacks and can heal other monsters. Barghests are fast melee attackers. Ettins are large, powerful bruisers that block two spaces. Elementals hit hard. Merriods lurk in water. Shadow Dragons are elite threats. Learn each monster's strengths and use them accordingly.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Quest Guide Carefully
walkthrough.text: Every quest has unique rules, objectives, and special conditions that override the standard rules. Read the Quest Guide thoroughly before playing. Pay attention to victory conditions for both sides, special monster rules, when reinforcements appear, and what happens when doors open. Many games are lost because a player did not notice a critical quest rule. The Quest Guide is the most important document in any game of Descent. When in doubt, the quest rules always take precedence over the rulebook.

walkthrough.title: Hero Composition and Synergy
walkthrough.text: Choosing heroes that complement each other is the first step to a strong team. A balanced party typically includes at least one melee fighter (Warrior archetype), one healer (Healer archetype), and one ranged damage dealer (Scout or Mage). The fourth hero, if playing with four, should fill whatever gap remains. Consider class synergies: a Necromancer's Reanimate familiar can serve as an extra blocker and attacker, while a Disciple provides reliable healing. A Berserker deals massive damage but needs healing support. A Wildlander offers ranged flexibility and movement bonuses.

walkthrough.title: Maximizing the Move-Interrupt Rule
walkthrough.text: The ability to interrupt a move action with another action is one of the most powerful tactical tools in the game. Advanced players use this constantly. Move into range, attack, then move out of danger. Move adjacent to a search token, search, then continue past it. Move to a door, open it, then move into the newly revealed room. Move next to a knocked out hero, revive them, then keep moving. Every hero turn should plan around this flexibility. Combining this with fatigue-based extra movement points lets heroes cover enormous ground while still acting.

walkthrough.title: Optimal Use of Heroic Feats
walkthrough.text: Heroic feats are once per encounter, so timing is everything. Do not burn your feat in the first round unless the situation is desperate. The ideal time is during a critical push: opening a door into a room full of monsters, making a run for an objective, or saving a knocked out hero in a dire position. Since feats refresh between encounters, there is no reason to save them for Encounter 2. Use your feat to swing Encounter 1 in your favor. Some feats work best reactively, like a defensive feat when the overlord commits monsters. Others are best used proactively, like a movement feat to reach an objective.

walkthrough.title: Overlord Deck Optimization (Campaign)
walkthrough.text: In a campaign, the overlord can customize their deck between quests. The three upgrade classes are Warlord, Saboteur, and Magus. Warlord cards buff monsters directly. Saboteur cards feature traps and disruption. Magus cards offer magical effects. Level 1 cards cost 1 XP, Level 2 cost 2 XP (require two Level 1 cards of that class), and Level 3 cost 3 XP (require three cards of that class). A focused strategy in one class is often stronger than spreading across all three. Before each quest, the overlord can remove cards from the deck to optimize, keeping a minimum of 15 cards. Tailor your deck to counter the specific heroes you face.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Economy: Gold and Shopping
walkthrough.text: In a campaign, gold management is critical. Heroes earn gold from Search card values and quest rewards. They share a collective gold pool and shop after each quest. The shop reveals one card per hero plus one additional card from the Shop Item deck. Heroes can buy any number they can afford. Unused Act I cards can still be bought at the transition to Act II, but after that, only Act II items appear. Key insight: starting equipment can be sold for 25 gold each. Sell outdated gear to fund powerful upgrades. Also, the 2-for-1 rule means heroes should pool gold strategically rather than each hero buying cheap items.

walkthrough.title: Controlling Chokepoints
walkthrough.text: Map awareness wins games for both sides. Doorways, narrow corridors, and bridge tiles create natural chokepoints. Heroes should use chokepoints defensively: position a tough Warrior in a doorway to absorb damage while ranged heroes attack safely from behind. The overlord should use chokepoints offensively: block the heroes' path to the objective with large monsters that take multiple attacks to remove. A single ettin in a corridor can stall the entire hero party for two or three rounds. Learn to identify chokepoints on each quest map during setup.

walkthrough.title: Advanced Combat: Surge Priority
walkthrough.text: Surges are precious. When you roll surges during an attack, you must decide how to spend them. The priority depends on the situation. If you are making a Ranged attack that barely reaches the target, spending a surge on extra range prevents a miss. If the target has high defense, Pierce is more valuable than raw damage. Condition-inflicting surges like Stun can be game-changing because they deny the target an action. For heroes, remember one unused surge can recover one fatigue. Sometimes recovering fatigue is more valuable than the extra damage, especially early in a quest when you need sustain.

walkthrough.title: The Act I to Act II Transition
walkthrough.text: The transition from Act I to Act II in a campaign is a major power shift. Monsters and lieutenants switch to Act II cards with stronger stats and abilities. The Shop Item deck switches to Act II with more powerful equipment. This transition happens after the Interlude quest. Heroes should spend their gold wisely before the transition because all unpurchased Act I items go back in the box. The overlord should be aware that Act II monsters are significantly stronger, allowing more aggressive play. Which Interlude quest is played depends on who won more Act I quests, so every quest matters.

walkthrough.title: Positioning for Large Monsters
walkthrough.text: Large monsters (Ettins, Elementals, Merriods, Shadow Dragons) occupy more than one space and have unique movement rules. They shrink to one space for counting movement, then expand when they stop. This means they can squeeze through single-space corridors during movement but need enough open space to end their turn. Smart overlord play involves threading large monsters through tight spaces to reach heroes in open rooms. Smart hero play involves cornering large monsters where they cannot expand, effectively trapping them in suboptimal positions.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Meta: Quest Selection in Campaigns
walkthrough.text: In a campaign, the winner of each quest chooses the next quest from those available. This creates a metagame. Each Act I quest maps to two possible Act II quests: one if the heroes won, one if the overlord won. Winning Act I quests gives the heroes more favorable Act II options. The Interlude and Finale quests are also determined by who won more quests in their respective act. If the heroes win at least two of three Act I quests, they play a more favorable Interlude. The same applies for Act II leading to the Finale. Every quest victory compounds across the entire campaign. Play to win every quest, but also consider which quests benefit your side more when choosing.

entries.definition: The player who controls all monsters, lieutenants, and Overlord cards. The overlord opposes the hero players and has their own victory conditions for each quest. One player is always the overlord.

A player-controlled character that ventures into dungeons, fights monsters, and completes quest objectives cooperatively with other heroes. Each hero has a Hero sheet with stats, an archetype, a unique ability, and a heroic feat.
One of four hero archetypes. Warriors are tough melee fighters. Available classes: Knight and Berserker. Warriors typically have high Health and Might.
One of four hero archetypes. Healers support the party with recovery abilities. Available classes: Disciple and Spiritspeaker. Healers typically have high Willpower.
One of four hero archetypes. Mages deal damage from range using magical abilities. Available classes: Runemaster and Necromancer. Mages typically have high Knowledge.
One of four hero archetypes. Scouts are fast, agile, and excel at ranged combat and searching. Available classes: Thief and Wildlander. Scouts typically have high Awareness and Speed.
A Warrior class focused on defense and protecting allies. Knights can absorb damage meant for adjacent heroes and use shields effectively. Starting equipment includes a melee weapon and shield.

A Warrior class focused on aggressive melee damage. Berserkers deal extra damage by suffering fatigue, trading their own endurance for devastating attacks. Starting equipment includes a two-handed weapon.
A Healer class focused on direct healing and support. Disciples can heal adjacent heroes and provide sustained recovery throughout encounters. Starting equipment includes a melee weapon and shield.
A Healer class focused on area healing and spiritual powers. Spiritspeakers can heal multiple heroes and manipulate conditions. Starting equipment includes a magic-based weapon.

A Mage class focused on powerful ranged magical attacks. Runemasters use runic weapons to deal heavy damage from a distance with Blast and other keywords. Starting equipment includes a runic ranged weapon.

A Mage class that commands a Reanimate familiar. The Reanimate is a token placed on the map that can move and attack like a figure. It blocks line of sight and movement, and can be targeted by monster attacks. The Necromancer controls it during their turn.
A Scout class focused on stealth, traps, and treasure. Thieves excel at searching and can use heroic abilities to avoid damage. Starting equipment includes a light ranged weapon.
A Scout class focused on ranged combat and mobility. Wildlanders have skills that enhance ranged attacks and movement, making them excellent at hit-and-run tactics. Starting equipment includes a bow.
A Scout hero with high Speed (5) and Awareness. Known for her mobility and ranged prowess. Often paired with the Wildlander or Thief class. Her heroic feat enhances her ability to move and attack.
A Scout hero, a small and nimble rogue. Known for his evasiveness and stealth capabilities. His attributes favor Awareness, making him excellent at avoiding traps.
A Healer hero, a devout warrior-priest. Known for his supportive abilities and solid combat presence. His heroic feat provides healing to nearby allies, making him a reliable party anchor.
A Mage hero, a scholarly wizard. Known for his high Knowledge attribute and powerful ranged attacks. Often paired with the Runemaster class for maximum magical damage.

A Warrior hero, a dwarven fighter. Known for his high Health and Might, making him extremely tough in melee combat. His heroic feat can devastate nearby enemies. Often paired with the Berserker class.
A Warrior hero, an elven knight. Known for balanced stats and a versatile heroic feat. Often paired with the Knight class for a defensive, party-protecting role.
A Healer hero with a focus on Willpower. Known for her ability to support the party through spiritual powers. Works well with the Spiritspeaker class for area healing and condition management.

A Mage hero, a mystical shaman. Known for her connection to familiars and dark magic. Often paired with the Necromancer class to command the Reanimate familiar. Has strong Knowledge and Willpower attributes.

A monster type. Zombies are slow (low Speed) but durable melee fighters. The base game includes 4 tan minions and 1 red master. They make excellent blockers due to their high Health relative to their low cost. Melee attackers.

A monster type. Goblin Archers are fast, ranged attackers that harass heroes from a distance. The base game includes 4 tan minions and 1 red master. Individually fragile but dangerous in groups. Ranged attackers.
A monster type. Cave Spiders are numerous, fast, and can inflict conditions. The base game includes 4 tan minions and 1 red master. They swarm heroes and can Poison targets. Melee attackers.

A monster type. Flesh Moulders are ranged attackers with the ability to heal other monsters. The base game includes 3 tan minions and 1 red master. Keep them behind frontline monsters to heal and deal damage. Ranged attackers.
A monster type. Barghests are fast, wolf-like melee attackers. The base game includes 3 tan minions and 1 red master. They can Stun heroes, denying them actions. Melee attackers with high Speed.

A large monster type occupying more than one space. Ettins are powerful two-headed bruisers. The base game includes 1 tan minion and 1 red master. They block two spaces, making them excellent for corridor control. Melee attackers with high Health.

A large monster type. Elementals are powerful elemental beings with strong attacks. The base game includes 1 tan minion and 1 red master. They deal heavy damage and occupy multiple spaces. Ranged attackers.

A large monster type. Merriods are tentacled creatures that thrive in water terrain. The base game includes 1 tan minion and 1 red master. They have abilities that interact with water spaces. Melee attackers.

A large monster type. Shadow Dragons are the most powerful standard monsters in the base game. The base game includes 1 tan minion and 1 red master. They have high stats across the board and elite abilities. Melee attackers.
The basic variety of a monster, represented by tan figures. Minions have lower stats and fewer abilities than masters. The Monster card shows minion information in the upper section.

The elite variety of a monster, represented by red figures. Masters have higher Speed, Health, and Defense than minions, plus stronger abilities. The Monster card shows master information in the lower section.

A powerful named character controlled by the overlord, represented by a token on the map. Lieutenants are treated as monster figures for all purposes but have attributes and can make attribute tests like heroes. They have both Act I and Act II cards. Each lieutenant is its own monster group.

A hero action that grants movement points equal to the hero's Speed. Moving into an adjacent space costs 1 movement point (terrain may cost more). Can be interrupted by another action. Monsters also perform move actions during their activation.

A hero action using an equipped weapon to attack a space containing a monster. Follows the 5-step combat sequence: Declare, Roll, Check Range, Spend Surges, Deal Damage. Monsters may only attack once per activation.
A hero action that recovers all fatigue at the end of the hero's turn. Place a hero token on the Activation card as a reminder. At end of turn, discard all fatigue tokens from the Hero sheet.

A hero action performed when adjacent to or in a space with a search token. Flip the token face up and draw the top Search card. Unique search tokens have quest-specific effects described in the Quest Guide instead.

The only action a knocked out hero may perform. Roll 2 red power dice, recover damage equal to hearts rolled and fatigue equal to surges rolled, replace hero token with figure, then flip Activation card (no second action). If the space is occupied, place figure in nearest empty space.

A hero action to help a knocked out hero in an adjacent space. The knocked out hero rolls 2 red power dice, recovers damage equal to hearts and fatigue equal to surges, then replaces their hero token with their figure.

entries.term: Open or Close a Door Action
entries.definition: A hero or monster action to open or close one adjacent door. When opened, the door token is removed from the map. Figures cannot move or trace line of sight through closed doors. Spaces separated by a door are not adjacent.
A hero action to activate a Class card ability marked with an action icon. The hero follows the rules on the card and pays any fatigue cost. If the skill allows an attack, normal attack rules apply.
A characteristic on Hero sheets and Monster cards representing how many movement points a figure receives when performing a move action. Heroes can suffer fatigue for extra movement; monsters cannot.

A characteristic representing the total damage a figure can suffer before being defeated. When damage tokens on a figure equal or exceed its Health, it is defeated. Heroes are knocked out; monsters are removed.

A hero-only characteristic representing the maximum amount of fatigue a hero can voluntarily suffer. If forced fatigue would exceed Stamina, the excess becomes damage instead. Monsters have no Stamina; any fatigue they would suffer becomes damage.

A characteristic showing which defense dice a figure rolls when attacked. Listed on the Hero sheet or Monster card. Common values include one gray die, one brown die, or combinations of gray and black dice.

A hero attribute representing strength and toughness. Tested by rolling 1 gray and 1 black defense die. Pass if shields rolled are equal to or less than the attribute value. Used for physical challenges and resisting physical effects.

A hero attribute representing education and intelligence. Tested the same way as Might. Used for magical challenges, solving puzzles, and resisting mental effects. Mages typically have high Knowledge.
A hero attribute representing mental constitution and discipline. Used for resisting fear effects, dark magic, and other willpower-based challenges. Healers typically have high Willpower.
A hero attribute representing dexterity, vigilance, and perception. Used for noticing traps, avoiding ambushes, and agility-based challenges. Scouts typically have high Awareness.
A condition inflicted by certain attacks (requires at least 1 damage after defense). While diseased, the hero cannot recover any damage from any source. Refer to the Condition card for removal rules.

A condition inflicted by certain attacks (requires at least 1 damage after defense). While immobilized, the figure cannot perform move actions or suffer fatigue to gain movement points. Refer to the Condition card for removal rules.

A condition inflicted by certain attacks (requires at least 1 damage after defense). While poisoned, the figure suffers penalties to its attributes or other stats. Refer to the Condition card for specific effects and removal rules.

A condition inflicted by certain attacks (requires at least 1 damage after defense). A stunned figure must use an action to discard the Stun condition before performing other actions. This effectively costs the figure one of its actions.
Terrain marked by a red border. Obstacles are blocked spaces that prevent movement and block line of sight. Figures cannot enter or move through obstacle spaces.

Terrain marked by a blue border. Entering a water space costs 2 movement points instead of the normal 1. Large monsters ending movement adjacent to water can place their base in water without penalty. Familiars treat special terrain as water.

Terrain marked by a green border. Entering a pit deals 2 damage, ends the move action, and limits line of sight to adjacent figures only. Requires a special action to climb out. Figures cannot jump over pit spaces. Large monsters are only affected if all occupied spaces are pit spaces.

Terrain marked by a yellow border. Entering a lava space immediately deals 1 damage. Any figure ending its turn on lava is immediately defeated. Heroes defeated by lava place their hero token in the nearest non-lava empty space. Large monsters are only defeated if all occupied spaces are lava.

A space that contains a figure or obstacle. Blocked spaces prevent movement and block line of sight. However, figures may move diagonally around corners and between two blocked spaces. Friendly figures do not block movement for each other.

The primary attack die rolled in every attack. Shows hearts (damage), numbers (range), surges, and one X (miss) face. An X means the attack misses regardless of all other results. There is 1 blue die in the game.

A power die that adds hearts (damage) and surges to attacks. Rolled alongside the blue attack die when specified by a weapon or Monster card. There are 2 red power dice in the game. Also rolled for Stand Up and Revive actions.
A power die that adds hearts (damage), range, and surges to attacks. Provides a mix of damage and range. There are 2 yellow power dice in the game.
The weakest defense die, providing fewer shield results. There is 1 brown defense die in the game.
A standard defense die providing moderate shield results. Also used in attribute tests along with the black die. There are 2 gray defense dice in the game.
The strongest defense die, providing the most shield results. Also used in attribute tests along with the gray die. There is 1 black defense die in the game.

A dice symbol representing damage dealt on an attack roll, or damage recovered during recovery actions (like Stand Up or Revive). Total hearts from the attack minus shields from defense equals damage dealt.

A dice symbol on defense dice that cancels one heart (damage) from an attack. Each shield cancels exactly one damage. Excess shields are ignored. Defense dice are only rolled during attacks unless otherwise specified.

A dice symbol that can trigger special abilities on weapons, skills, and Monster cards. Each surge can trigger one ability, and each ability can only trigger once per attack. One unused surge per attack can recover 1 fatigue for heroes. Monsters cannot use surges to recover fatigue.
A symbol on the blue attack die. If rolled, the attack is a miss and ALL other dice results are ignored. No damage, no surges, no effects. The attack simply fails.

Numbers on attack and power dice representing the maximum distance a Ranged attack can reach. Count spaces from attacker to target; the total range rolled must equal or exceed this distance. Melee attacks do not require range.

An attack keyword. All spaces adjacent to the targeted space are also affected. One attack roll applies to all affected figures, but each figure rolls defense separately. Only the target space needs range and line of sight. Affects both friendly and enemy figures.
An attack keyword. Ignores a number of shield results from the defense roll equal to the Pierce rating. Pierce from multiple sources stack (Pierce 1 + Pierce 2 = ignore 3 shields).
An attack keyword. Allows a Melee attack to target a figure up to 2 spaces away instead of only adjacent spaces. The target must still be in line of sight.

The ability to see a target space. Trace a straight line from any corner of the figure's space to any corner of the target. Blocked by map tile edges, doors, figures, and obstacles. Touching only a corner of a blocked space does NOT block line of sight. Adjacent spaces are always in line of sight.

Any space sharing an edge or corner with another space. All adjacent spaces are in line of sight. Spaces separated by a door are NOT adjacent. Many actions (Melee attacks, Search, Revive, Open Door) require adjacency.

Some cards require exhausting (rotating sideways) when used. An exhausted card cannot be used again until refreshed. All exhausted cards are refreshed (rotated upright) at the start of the controlling player's turn.

When a hero suffers damage equal to or exceeding their Health, they are knocked out. Replace figure with hero token, suffer fatigue up to Stamina and damage up to Health, discard Conditions, overlord draws 1 card. Can only Stand Up next turn. Immune to attacks. Hero token does not block movement or line of sight.

A powerful ability unique to each hero, listed on the Hero sheet. Can be used once per encounter. After use, flip the Hero sheet face down. Refreshes at the start of the next encounter. Some require an action to perform.

A test of a hero's Might, Knowledge, Willpower, or Awareness. Roll 1 gray defense die and 1 black defense die. Pass if shields rolled are equal to or less than the attribute value. Monsters automatically fail. Lieutenants test normally.

A creature controlled by a hero, represented by a token. Activated once per hero turn (before or after hero actions, no action required). Can move up to its Speed. Does not normally block line of sight or movement. Some familiars (like the Reanimate) are treated as figures and CAN be attacked.

Bridge-sized cards representing the overlord's powers. The basic deck has 15 cards. No cost to play, no hand limit. Each specifies when it can be played. Same-named cards cannot target the same figure for the same trigger. Overlord draws 1 card per turn. Shuffle discard when deck runs out.

One of three overlord upgrade card classes available in campaign play. Warlord cards focus on buffing monsters and enhancing their combat abilities. Contains Level 1, 2, and 3 cards with increasing power and cost.
One of three overlord upgrade card classes available in campaign play. Saboteur cards focus on traps, disruption, and interfering with hero actions. Contains Level 1, 2, and 3 cards.
One of three overlord upgrade card classes available in campaign play. Magus cards focus on magical effects and dark sorcery. Contains Level 1, 2, and 3 cards.

The separate book included with the game containing all 20 quests with setup diagrams, special rules, victory conditions, encounter transitions, and the campaign map. Quest rules take precedence over the rulebook.
A double-sided card for each hero showing their name, archetype, Speed, Health, Stamina, Defense, four attributes, hero ability, and heroic feat. Flipped face down after using heroic feat.

A card showing all stats and abilities for a monster type. Top section: minion info. Bottom section: master info. Back shows monster group size by hero count, trait icons, and detailed ability rules. Both Act I and Act II versions exist.

A card given to each hero player. One side shows the hero turn summary; the other shows the overlord turn summary. Flipped face down when a hero finishes their turn. Flipped face up at end of overlord turn to start a new round.

Cards drawn when a hero performs a search action on a non-unique search token. Include items like Health Potions and other one-time-use effects. In campaigns, also provide gold value. The deck contains 12 cards.

Cards belonging to one of the 8 hero classes. Include skills and starting equipment. Basic cards have no XP cost. Advanced skills show an experience point cost and are purchased during campaigns. 84 total Class cards across all 8 classes.

Cards representing equipment heroes can purchase during campaigns. Divided into Act I (20 cards) and Act II (14 cards) decks. Show name, equip icons (hand/armor/other), dice, cost in gold, traits, and special rules.

Double-sided cards representing unique, powerful campaign items. One side for heroes, one for the overlord. Cannot be bought or sold — only earned as quest rewards. Overlord relics are wielded by lieutenants. 6 Relic cards total.

Double-sided puzzle-cut tokens used to build the quest map. One side shows indoor locations, the other outdoor environments. Each tile has a unique code (number + letter) for identification. 48 map tiles total.

A distinct phase within a quest with its own map layout, rules, and objectives. Most quests have 2 encounters. Winning Encounter 1 does not determine the quest winner. Between encounters, heroes keep damage but recover fatigue.

A series of 9 linked quests played over multiple sessions (approximately 20 hours total). Consists of: Introduction, 3 Act I quests, Interlude, 3 Act II quests, and Finale. Players earn experience points and gold between quests. The Shadow Rune is the base game campaign.

The campaign included in the base game of Descent: Journeys in the Dark Second Edition. Set in the baronies of Rhynn and Carthridge on the outskirts of Terrinoth. Played over 9 quests with branching paths based on quest outcomes.

The first half of a campaign. Uses Act I Monster cards, Lieutenant cards, and Shop Item cards. Consists of the Introduction, 3 chosen Act I quests, and the Interlude. Quest outcomes determine which Act II quests become available.

The second half of a campaign. Uses stronger Act II Monster cards, Lieutenant cards, and Shop Item cards. Consists of 3 Act II quests and the Finale. Available Act II quests depend on who won each Act I quest.

A special quest marking the transition from Act I to Act II. Two possible Interludes exist. If heroes won 2+ Act I quests, play The Shadow Vault. If overlord won 2+, play The Overlord Revealed. Considered an Act I quest.

The final quest of a campaign, played after 3 Act II quests. Two possible Finales exist. If heroes won 2+ Act II quests, play Gryvorn Unleashed. If overlord won 2+, play The Man Who Would Be King. The Finale winner wins the campaign.

Points earned after each quest (1 XP per player regardless of outcome). Heroes spend XP on Class deck skills. The overlord spends XP on upgrade Overlord cards (Warlord/Saboteur/Magus). XP cannot be pooled or transferred between players.

Currency earned by heroes from Search card values and quest rewards. Heroes share a collective gold pool. Spent during Shopping to buy Shop Item cards. Starting equipment can be sold for 25 gold each. Other items sell for half value rounded down to nearest 25.

A Campaign phase step where heroes buy equipment. Shuffle the current act's Shop Item deck and reveal cards equal to number of heroes + 1. Heroes may buy any they can afford. Unpurchased cards are shuffled back.

A Campaign phase step where heroes travel to the next quest location using the campaign map. The overlord draws Travel Event cards for each icon along the path. Events may cause damage, fatigue, or conditions that carry into the quest.

An optional variant for playing single quests with higher-level characters. Three levels: Basic (normal setup), Advanced (3 XP + 150 gold for heroes, 4 XP for overlord), Expert (6 XP + 250 gold for heroes, 8 XP for overlord with Act II cards).

In a two-player game, one player is the overlord and the other controls two heroes. Each hero takes their turn independently and is treated as if controlled by a different player. The same player makes all decisions for both heroes.

An attack type that can only target adjacent spaces (unless the weapon has Reach). Shown by a crossed-swords icon on weapons and Monster cards. Heroes attacking with bare hands use a Melee attack with just the blue attack die.

An attack type that can target any space in line of sight. Shown by a crosshair/bow icon on weapons and Monster cards. The attacker must roll enough range on the dice to reach the target, or the attack misses.

A monster figure occupying more than one space on the map (Ettins, Elementals, Merriods, Shadow Dragons). For movement, the overlord picks one occupied space and counts from there as if one space. When stopping, the figure expands to fill its base. Can change orientation but must fit entirely on the map.

Heroes can equip a maximum of 2 hand icons worth of items, 1 Armor item, and 2 Other items. Cards without equip icons can be equipped without restriction. Unequipped items are flipped face down and cannot be used.

During a move action, a hero may trade any number of Shop Item, Relic, and Search cards with an adjacent hero. Trading does not cost an action or movement points. Heroes cannot trade Class cards or starting equipment. Newly received items cannot be equipped until the start of the next turn.
